08/09/2025, 5:06 pm - iehehei: 1. NEO-PI-R (Five-Factor Model)

a) Church, Katigbak, & del Pilar et al. (Late 1990s–2000s)
One-liner: Filipino-language NEO-PI-R shows solid internal consistency (.79–.91) and confirms the Five-Factor Model works in Filipino samples.
APA citation:
McCrae, R. R., Costa, P. T., Jr., del Pilar, G., Rolland, J., & Parker, W. D. (1998). Filipino (Tagalog) translation and validation of the NEO Personality Inventory-Revised (NEO-PI-R). In A. T. Church (Ed.), Culture and the behavioral manifestations of traits: Validity of the five-factor model in the Philippines (pp. …). [Article]. One-liner based on internal consistency data (.79–.91) .

b) Katigbak, Church, Guanzon-Lapeña, Carlota, & del Pilar (2002)
One-liner: The FFM replicates well in Philippine samples, though some indigenous traits offer incremental validity beyond FFM.
APA citation:
Katigbak, M. S., Church, A. T., Guanzon-Lapeña, M. A., Carlota, A. J., & del Pilar, G. (2002). Are indigenous personality dimensions culture-specific? Journal of Personality and Social Psychology, 82(1), 1–17. .

c) Church & Katigbak (2002)
One-liner: NEO-PI-R’s factor structure generalizes well when using targeted rotations in Filipino samples.
APA citation:
Church, A. T., & Katigbak, M. S. (2002). The cross-cultural generalizability of personality traits: Philippine samples of the Revised NEO Personality Inventory. Journal of Cross-Cultural Psychology, 33(3), 558–573. .


---

2. Sixteen Personality Factor Test (16-PF)

a) Ambag, Viray, & Viray (2015)
One-liner: In PUP students, correlations between 16-PF traits and learning-style dimensions were mostly negative, with only a few small positive associations.
APA citation:
Ambag, S. C., Viray, J. R. B., & Viray, K. (2015). Correlation between personality types (16-PF) and learning styles of selected Polytechnic University of the Philippines students: Basis for learning enhancement program. European Academic Research, III(4), 5027–5039. .


---

3. MMPI (Minnesota Multiphasic Personality Inventory)

a) Dalupan (1995)
One-liner: The Filipino-language MMPI translation had adequate reliability and strong test–retest stability across versions.
APA citation:
Dalupan, M. A. (1995). Evaluating the translation adequacy of a Filipino-language MMPI: A reliability study. Philippine Journal of Psychology, 28(2), 1–25. .

b) Alcuaz-Reyes (1981) via Cheung et al. (2003)
One-liner: Early Tagalog MMPI translations had notable issues, but MMPI-2 (English version with US norms) became prevalent for clinical and screening uses in the Philippines.
APA citation:
Cheung, F. M., Butcher, J. N., & Lim, J. (2003). Use of the MMPI-2 with Asian populations. Psychological Assessment, 15(3), 248–256. (Based on Philippines context and translation history) .

08/09/2025, 9:34 pm - iehehei: Commonly Used Tests

Mini-Mental State Examination (MMSE): A widely used test for general cognitive screening. 

Montreal Cognitive Assessment (MoCA): Another popular screening tool for assessing cognitive function. 

Saint Louis University Mental Status (SLUMS) Exam: An assessment tool for evaluating cognitive status. 

Rey Auditory Verbal Learning Test (RAVLT): A test that assesses verbal learning and memory. 

Rey-Osterrieth Complex Figure Test: A test that evaluates visuospatial ability and memory by assessing the ability to copy and recall a complex figure.
